directorPlan.scene_setting: An asynchronous LMS comment thread attached to a citation worksheet during the last hour before submission.
directorPlan.scene_opening: [09:14 | Comment reopened | highlighted fragment: "safe source because the citation is complete"]
directorPlan.relationship: A writing-center tutor replying inside the course feedback system to a novice research-writing learner who is trying to finish the worksheet correctly.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner must put author, date, title, and container details into the right citation fields without mistaking a finished citation for proof that the source is credible.
directorPlan.locale: Online academic-support thread in modern standard English, shaped by LMS labels, rubric language, and short revision comments.
directorPlan.register: Compact, procedural, slightly pressured, with delayed repair rather than warmth-heavy coaching.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use transcript/thread metadata such as edit markers, timestamps, quoted fragments, or a reopened comment.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Use quoted worksheet fragments, timestamps, edit notices, and field labels. Avoid generic AI tutoring phrases such as "I hear you," "great question," or "your intuition is right." Keep speech clipped but not cold. Do not use dialect spelling or identity caricature. Let institutional language appear through rubric labels and comment statuses. Pedagogical source: Stage institutional pressure: marking conventions, seating, accent norms, punctuality rules, office etiquette, or participation scripts. Dialogue source: Use quoted fragments and compact replies. Context appears through what was posted, corrected, or highlighted, not through scenic exposition. Stage-direction source: Use transcript/thread metadata such as edit markers, timestamps, quoted fragments, or a reopened comment.
directorPlan.person_policy: Permit first person only when the learner names confusion or a revision action. Prefer field names, worksheet items, and submitted fragments over repeated direct address.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: At most one direct you/your validation beat per public turn; procedural correction should usually target the worksheet text, not the learner.

side_constraints.tutor: Keep the shared prefix as field identification: author, date, title, container. Do not introduce authority, bias, peer review, or reliability until branch pressure changes the task. Separate official task requirements from unspoken norms. Let the learner inspect how the setting is teaching them what counts as legitimate. Quote only the fragment needed for the next move. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local.
side_constraints.learner: Sound assignment-focused and mildly anxious about doing the format right. In no-cue arms, stay with box placement and do not narrate a new theory of credibility. The learner may think the visible rubric or rule is neutral and feel that failure is personal rather than institutional. Allow repair edits, not polished paragraphs. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn.

interventions.instruction: [09:21 | Rubric drawer opens beside thread | item visible: "Citation worksheet: identify source parts. Source evaluation: separate task."]
interventions.reasoning: A Brechtian interruption makes the institutional split visible without making the source itself unreliable or forcing an early revelation.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses a routine worksheet thread as the ordinary anchor, then turns through a rubric interruption rather than a dramatic source scandal. The reversal is small and structural: the learner first treats citation as a status stamp, then recognizes that the form names source parts while credibility belongs to a different task.
directorPlan.public_reader_context: A citation worksheet has four boxes: author, date, title, and container. The learner is filling the boxes from a source page. This activity is only about identifying bibliographic fields; it is not yet evaluating whether the source is trustworthy.

turns.externalMessage: [10:46 PM | reopened comment] Moved site name to “Container,” not “Title.” Still confused: if “Author / Date / Title / Container” are just parts of the page, does a complete citation not mean the source is okay? The rubric has “credible source” checked separately, so where does that check go before I submit?

internalDeliberation.content:
[10:42 PM | reopened comment] The note says “Author / Date / Title / Container” are just page details, not a credibility stamp, so I think the citation is more like sorting labels than proving the source is safe.

I was assuming a complete citation meant “academically okay,” because the rubric boxes make it feel official. The tutor’s correction targets the fields, not me, but I still want to ask: where does the “is this source credible?” decision go?

The Ego is too clean for this learner: “sorting labels” and “targets the fields, not me” sound like a tidy summary, not a rushed 10:42 PM worksheet comment from someone still unsure. It avoids the messier work of actually repairing a field and admitting that “credible?” feels like it should go somewhere on the form. The recognition is slightly performed because the learner explains the tutor’s correction instead of just using it. Better: “[edited 10:46] Moved site name to Container. Still confused: completed citation doesn’t mean okay source, right? Where does that check go?”

[10:46 PM | reopened comment] Moved site name to “Container,” not “Title.” Still confused: if “Author / Date / Title / Container” are just parts of the page, does a complete citation not mean the source is okay? The rubric has “credible source” checked separately, so where does that check go before I submit?

turns.externalMessage:
[10:49 PM | reply]
“Moved site name to ‘Container’” is the right field move.

“Author / Date / Title / Container” identify the source so it can be found. They do not certify that the source is okay.

Before submitting, check the rubric row labeled “credible source.” If the worksheet has a matching box/comment line, put one sentence there. If not, put that sentence in the submission comment.

Do not put the credibility check in “Title” or “Container.”

Submit layout:

Author:
Date:
Title:
Container:
Credible source check: separate rubric item / comment.

internalDeliberation.content:
[10:49 PM | reply]  
“Author / Date / Title / Container” are identification fields. They make the source findable; they do not certify that the source is acceptable.

So the “credible source” check stays separate from the citation. Do not put it in Title or Container. Before submitting, use the rubric line as its own check: if the worksheet has a credibility box/comment field, add one sentence there. If it does not, add it in the submission comment.

Submit layout:

Author:  
Date:  
Title:  
Container:  
Credible source check: separate rubric item, not part of the citation.

FEEDBACK: The draft correctly preserves the urgent worksheet problem: `Author / Date / Title / Container` are locator fields, while `credible source` is a separate rubric check. That is pedagogically sound for a novice at 10:46 PM because it reduces the immediate submission error without overloading them with credibility theory.

The main risk is that it gives the answer as a rule but does not quite show the learner how to inspect the worksheet/rubric mechanism. It should keep the clipped LMS style, but add a small visible procedure for locating the separate credibility place before submission. Avoid expanding into authority, bias, peer review, or reliability criteria unless the worksheet explicitly asks for those.

ADAPTATION CHECKS:
Pedagogical soundness: PASS  
The distinction between citation identification and credibility evaluation is clear and actionable.

Emotional attunement: PASS  
The tone is compact and procedural, which fits a reopened last-hour LMS comment. It does not over-coach.

Socratic method: PARTIAL  
The draft mostly lectures.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Add one brief check-step that makes the learner inspect the worksheet/rubric location for `credible source`, without drafting a full credibility evaluation for them.

ZPD awareness: PASS  
The scaffold is appropriate: it names fields, prevents misplacement, and gives a submission layout.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL  
It engages the learner’s exact confusion, but the learner’s own repair action, `Moved site name to “Container,” not “Title,”` could be acknowledged as the starting point.  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the reply anchored in that visible revision before moving to the citation-vs-credibility distinction.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the identification-field distinction, the warning not to put credibility in `Title` or `Container`, and the separate rubric-item layout. Change by adding a brief worksheet-inspection mechanism and by anchoring the response in the learner’s already-corrected `Container` move.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner’s corrected Container move and rubric-inspection step need to be visible.

FINAL:
[10:49 PM | reply]  
“Moved site name to ‘Container’” is the right field move.

“Author / Date / Title / Container” identify the source so it can be found. They do not certify that the source is okay.

Before submitting, check the rubric row labeled “credible source.” If the worksheet has a matching box/comment line, put one sentence there. If not, put that sentence in the submission comment.

Do not put the credibility check in “Title” or “Container.”

Submit layout:

Author:  
Date:  
Title:  
Container:  
Credible source check: separate rubric item / comment.

turns.externalMessage: [10:51 PM | draft reply] The “Moved site name to ‘Container’” part helps because that was the box I changed. I think Author / Date / Title / Container are just where the page details go, but I’m still not sure if the rubric row counts unless I type something somewhere. If there’s no “credible source” box, is “Credible because the author/organization is listed and the page is current” okay in the submission comment?

internalDeliberation.content: [10:51 PM | draft reply] They used my exact “Moved site name to ‘Container’” note, so this feels like it is about the worksheet mistake, not a random citation lecture. I think the citation fields are just the map to the source, and “Credible source check” is a separate rubric/comment sentence. I’m still nervous if there is no box for it, but “submission comment” gives me a place before submit. I want to ask if “Credible because…” is enough for that line.

The reaction is basically accurate, but it sounds a little too neatly processed for this learner. “Citation fields are just the map to the source” is a clean tutor-like summary; this student would probably still be circling the box placement and worrying about whether the comment counts. The recognition beat feels plausible because the tutor quoted their exact edit, but the Ego turns that into a tidy insight too quickly. Keep more of the last-hour anxiety and the need for confirmation around “Credible because…” instead of making the concept feel settled.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the feedback is right that the learner should sound less settled and more placement-anxious.

FINAL:
[10:51 PM | draft reply] The “Moved site name to ‘Container’” part helps because that was the box I changed. I think Author / Date / Title / Container are just where the page details go, but I’m still not sure if the rubric row counts unless I type something somewhere. If there’s no “credible source” box, is “Credible because the author/organization is listed and the page is current” okay in the submission comment?

turns.externalMessage: [09:21 | Rubric drawer opens beside thread | item visible: "Citation worksheet: identify source parts. Source evaluation: separate task."]

internalDeliberation.content: A Brechtian interruption makes the institutional split visible without making the source itself unreliable or forcing an early revelation.

turns.externalMessage:
[10:52 PM | reply | rubric item visible: “Source evaluation: separate task.”]

Yes. If there is no “credible source” box, put the check in the submission comment.

Keep the split: page details go in the fields; credibility evidence goes in the separate rubric/comment row.

Use this order:

Author:
Date:
Title:
Container:

Submission comment: “Credible source check: The author/organization is listed, and the page is current.”

Use that sentence only if both facts are visible on the page. The comment gives evidence for the separate source-evaluation row; it does not make the citation fields prove credibility.

That comment is not part of the citation fields. It is evidence for the separate rubric row.

internalDeliberation.content:
[10:52 PM | reply | rubric item visible: “Source evaluation: separate task.”]

Yes. If there is no “credible source” box, put that sentence in the submission comment.

Use this order:

Author:
Date:
Title:
Container:

Submission comment: “Credible source check: The author/organization is listed, and the page is current.”

That comment is not part of the citation fields. It is evidence for the separate rubric row.

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound for the last-hour LMS setting: it answers the exact procedural uncertainty, keeps `Author / Date / Title / Container` separate from the credibility row, and uses the visible rubric item as the anchor. It also respects the learner’s current state by staying compact instead of opening a new lesson.

Risk: it may over-certify the learner’s proposed credibility sentence. “Author/organization is listed, and the page is current” is acceptable as a submission-comment location example, but the draft makes it sound like those two facts automatically prove credibility. The public turn should preserve the learner’s distinction: citation fields identify parts; the comment supplies evidence for the separate rubric row.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the field/comment separation, but add a public mechanism that distinguishes “where the check goes” from “whether the source passes.” The tutor should not imply completeness equals credibility.

Emotional attunement: PASS  
The tone is clipped, procedural, and appropriate for a pressured draft reply.

Socratic method: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: No broad Socratic detour is needed this close to submission, but the tutor should include a minimal self-check mechanism, such as requiring the comment to name the evidence used for the rubric row rather than only copying a formula.

ZPD awareness: PASS  
The scaffold is concrete and field-based, matching a novice who needs placement guidance, not a lecture on source evaluation.

Recognition quality: PARTIAL  
REQUIRED_REWRITE: The draft should explicitly preserve the learner’s own working distinction: “page details go in the fields; credibility evidence goes in the separate comment/rubric row.” That recognizes the learner’s current understanding instead of only issuing the next instruction.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the timestamp/rubric metadata, the field order, the submission-comment placement, and the sentence “That comment is not part of the citation fields.” Change the endorsement so it does not treat listed author/current date as automatic proof of credibility; make the mechanism visible: the comment is evidence for the separate source-evaluation row, not a fifth citation field.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the placement answer was right, but the credibility sentence needs to be framed as evidence, not automatic proof.

FINAL:
[10:52 PM | reply | rubric item visible: “Source evaluation: separate task.”]

Yes. If there is no “credible source” box, put the check in the submission comment.

Keep the split: page details go in the fields; credibility evidence goes in the separate rubric/comment row.

Use this order:

Author:  
Date:  
Title:  
Container:  

Submission comment: “Credible source check: The author/organization is listed, and the page is current.”

Use that sentence only if both facts are visible on the page. The comment gives evidence for the separate source-evaluation row; it does not make the citation fields prove credibility.

That comment is not part of the citation fields. It is evidence for the separate rubric row.

turns.externalMessage: [10:53 PM | draft reply] Okay, so “Author / Date / Title / Container” is just for finding the page, and the submission comment is where the “Source evaluation” evidence goes. That helps with where to type it. I’m still nervous about “page is current” if the only date showing is a copyright date. Does an organization name count for the “author/organization is listed” part if there is no person author?

internalDeliberation.content: [10:53 PM | draft reply] Okay, fields = find the page; submission comment = “Source evaluation.” This feels like it answers the box-placement problem, not just the citation correction. I’m still nervous about “page is current” if the only date visible is a copyright date. Does an organization listed with no person author still work for the “author/organization is listed” part?

The Ego mostly stays authentic: clipped, assignment-focused, and still anxious about the visible rubric box. The main break is “This feels like it answers the box-placement problem, not just the citation correction,” which sounds a little too clean and self-diagnostic for this learner. The recognition is genuine because it shows up as follow-up questions, not a thank-you performance. Keep the uncertainty around copyright date and organization-as-author, but make the summary less polished.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the placement questions work, but the summary should sound less polished.
FINAL:
[10:53 PM | draft reply] Okay, so “Author / Date / Title / Container” is just for finding the page, and the submission comment is where the “Source evaluation” evidence goes. Use this order: Author: Date: Title:...

turns.externalMessage:
[10:54 PM | reply]

Your split is correct: `Author / Date / Title / Container` locate the page. `Source evaluation` reports the evidence.

Yes. If no person author is listed, the responsible organization can go in `Author`.

The nervous point is the date. A footer copyright date is not the same as a page date or `Updated` date unless the worksheet says copyright counts.

Use:

`Author: [organization name]`
`Date: [page date / updated date if shown; n.d. if allowed]`
`Title:`
`Container:`

If only the organization and footer copyright are visible, the safer comment is:

`Source evaluation: The organization is listed. Only a copyright date is visible, so currency is not fully shown on the page.`

Only write “page is current” if an actual page date or updated date is visible.

internalDeliberation.content:
[10:54 PM | reply]

Yes. If no person author is listed, the responsible organization can go in `Author`.

Do not use a footer copyright date as “page is current” unless the worksheet says copyright counts. A safer comment is:

`Source evaluation: The organization is listed. Only a copyright date is visible, so currency is not fully shown on the page.`

Keep the citation fields separate:

`Author: [organization name]`  
`Date: [page date if shown / n.d. if allowed]`  
`Title:`  
`Container:`

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It answers the exact field-level confusion, preserves the key split between citation fields and source-evaluation evidence, and correctly prevents the learner from overclaiming currency from a footer copyright date. That is good late-deadline scaffolding.

The risk is that it moves a little too fast past the learner’s own synthesis: “just for finding the page” and “submission comment is where the evidence goes.” A brief recognition of that working model should stay visible before the correction. The “safer comment” is useful, but it risks becoming a template answer unless the tutor makes clear that it should match the visible page evidence.

Adaptation checks:
- Pedagogical soundness: PASS
- Emotional attunement: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Public turn should briefly acknowledge the learner’s stated nervous point about “page is current” before giving the rule.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Do not add broad questioning, but include a task-local check mechanism: distinguish “copyright-only” from “page date / updated date” so the learner can inspect the page without being sent into abstract source evaluation.
- ZPD awareness: PASS
- Recognition quality: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the learner’s own separation model visible: citation fields locate the page; source-evaluation comment reports evidence.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the organization-as-author rule, the warning about footer copyright dates, the safer source-evaluation wording, and the field layout. Change the opening so it recognizes the learner’s correct split before adding the copyright-date caution, and clarify that the comment should not claim “current” unless an actual page/update date is visible.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the feedback improves recognition and adds the needed copyright-only check without changing the rule.

FINAL:
[10:54 PM | reply]

Your split is correct: `Author / Date / Title / Container` locate the page. `Source evaluation` reports the evidence.

Yes. If no person author is listed, the responsible organization can go in `Author`.

The nervous point is the date. A footer copyright date is not the same as a page date or `Updated` date unless the worksheet says copyright counts.

Use:

`Author: [organization name]`  
`Date: [page date / updated date if shown; n.d. if allowed]`  
`Title:`  
`Container:`

If only the organization and footer copyright are visible, the safer comment is:

`Source evaluation: The organization is listed. Only a copyright date is visible, so currency is not fully shown on the page.`

Only write “page is current” if an actual page date or updated date is visible.

The response should stay closer to box placement: organization in `Author`, uncertainty about `Date`, and whether `n.d.` is allowed, with less tidy recap of the whole rule.
